home.title: China Guide - Your Ultimate China Travel Guide
home.metaDesc: Discover China with confidence. Expert travel guides, itineraries, and booking tools for American and English-speaking tourists.

hero.title: Discover the Real China
hero.subtitle: Ancient culture meets future cities. Your complete guide to traveling China with confidence.

whyChina.title: Why Visit China
whyChina.subtitle: From ancient wonders to modern marvels, China offers experiences unlike anywhere else on Earth
whyChina.cuisineDesc: Explore 8 legendary culinary traditions from Peking duck to Sichuan spicy dishes, Dim sum to street food markets.
whyChina.railDesc: Travel across vast distances comfortably on the world's largest high-speed rail network—fast, affordable, and scenic.
whyChina.historyDesc: Walk the Great Wall, explore the Forbidden City, and discover 5,000 years of continuous civilization.
whyChina.landscapeDesc: From Karst mountains in Guilin to the deserts of Xinjiang, China spans incredible geographic diversity.
whyChina.safetyDesc: China is one of the safest countries for tourists. Low crime rates, helpful locals, and modern infrastructure.
whyChina.cityDesc: Experience Shanghai's skyline, Shenzhen's tech innovation, and Beijing's blend of ancient and modern.

itineraries.title: Popular Itineraries
itineraries.subtitle: Curated travel routes designed for different interests and timeframes
itineraries.firstTimeDesc: The ultimate introduction to China's iconic destinations. Perfect for first-time visitors who want the highlights.
itineraries.ancientDesc: Follow the Silk Road heritage from Beijing through Xi'an to Dunhuang. A journey through 2,000 years of history.
itineraries.railDesc: Experience China's bullet trains connecting Shanghai, Nanjing, Suzhou, and beyond. Scenic and efficient.
itineraries.foodDesc: Spice up your trip with Chengdu's famous cuisine, Sichuan hot pot, and a panda base visit included.
itineraries.zhangDesc: Walk through Avatar-inspired sandstone pillars, glass bridges, and dramatic natural landscapes.

essentials.title: First Timer Essentials
essentials.subtitle: Everything you need to know before arriving in China
essentials.visaDesc: Visa requirements, application process, and 144-hour transit visa information.
essentials.paymentDesc: How to use Alipay and WeChat Pay as a foreigner with international cards.
essentials.vpnDesc: Internet access, VPN recommendations, and eSIM options for travelers.
essentials.appsDesc: Must-download apps for navigation, translation, and transportation.
essentials.faqDesc: Answers to the most common questions about traveling in China.

faq.title: Common Questions
faq.subtitle: Quick answers to help you plan your trip
faq.a1: Yes, China is generally very safe for tourists. violent crime is rare, and the country has extensive surveillance and a strong police presence. Pickpocketing can occur in crowded tourist areas, but overall you'll feel secure walking around at any hour. Locals are often helpful and curious about foreign visitors.
faq.q2: Do Americans need a visa for China?
faq.a2: Yes, most American citizens need a visa to enter China. However, Americans can use the 144-hour transit visa exemption if transiting through certain cities. For tourism purposes, you'll typically need to apply for a tourist visa (L visa) at your nearest Chinese embassy or consulate. The process takes about 4-5 business days.
faq.q3: Can I use Google, Facebook, and Instagram in China?
faq.a3: No, Google services (including search, maps, and Gmail), Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, YouTube, and many other Western websites and apps are blocked in China. We recommend installing a reliable VPN before arriving. Popular options include ExpressVPN and NordVPN, which work reasonably well in China.
faq.q4: Can foreigners use Alipay and WeChat Pay?
faq.a4: Yes, foreigners can now use Alipay and WeChat Pay with international credit cards (Visa, Mastercard, etc.). Simply download the apps, sign up with your passport, and link your foreign card. Most merchants in China prefer digital payments, so this is essential for a smooth experience.

faq.title: China Travel FAQs - China Guide
faq.metaDesc: Common questions about traveling to China. Visa, safety, payment, internet, and transportation FAQs.
faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know before visiting China
faq.q1: Do Americans need a visa for China?
faq.a1: Yes, most American citizens need a visa to enter China. However, US citizens can use the 144-hour transit visa exemption if passing through certain major cities. For tourism, you'll need to apply for a tourist visa (L visa) at a Chinese embassy or consulate. The process typically takes 4-5 business days.
faq.q2: How long does China visa approval take?
faq.a2: Standard processing is 4-5 business days. Express options are available (1-2 days) for an additional fee. We recommend applying at least 6-8 weeks before your trip to account for any delays.
faq.q3: Can I use the 144-hour transit visa to visit China?
faq.a3: Yes, if you are transiting through major Chinese cities (Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Chengdu, etc.) and continuing to a third country within 144 hours (6 days), you may qualify for this visa-free transit. Note that this only applies to specific entry points and doesn't work for Hong Kong.
faq.q4: Can foreigners use Alipay in China?
faq.a4: Yes! Foreigners can now use Alipay with international credit cards. Download the app, switch to English, select "Tourist" mode, scan your passport, and link your Visa, Mastercard, or Amex card. This is essential—most merchants in China don't accept cash or international cards.
faq.q5: Can I use my credit card in China?
faq.a5: International credit cards have very limited acceptance in China. They mainly work at large hotels, high-end restaurants, and major shopping malls. For everyday purchases, you'll need Alipay or WeChat Pay. Always carry some cash as backup.
faq.a6: No. Google services (search, maps, Gmail, Drive, YouTube) are all blocked in China by the Great Firewall. You'll need a VPN to access them. We recommend setting up ExpressVPN or NordVPN before arriving in China.
faq.q7: Do I need a VPN for China?
faq.a7: If you need to access Google services, Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, WhatsApp, or other blocked services, yes— you'll need a VPN. Download and install it before arriving. Popular options include ExpressVPN, NordVPN, and Surfshark. Note that VPN effectiveness can vary in China.
faq.q8: Will my phone work in China?
faq.a8: Most US phones will work in China through international roaming, but this can be expensive ($10-15/day). We recommend getting an eSIM or Chinese SIM card for affordable data. Make sure your phone is unlocked for international use.
faq.a9: Yes, China is generally very safe for tourists. Violent crime is rare, and the country has extensive security infrastructure. You may be approached by police for routine document checks— simply show your passport when requested. Petty crime like pickpocketing can occur in crowded tourist areas. Use normal precautions and you'll feel perfectly safe.
faq.a10: Yes, China is considered safe at night. Major cities have good street lighting, and you'll see police or security personnel in busy areas. It's common to see people walking alone late at night. As with any city, stay in well-lit, populated areas and use normal caution.
faq.q11: How do I buy train tickets in China?
faq.a11: You can buy tickets through the Trip.com app (English interface), the official 12306 app, or at train station counters. For high-speed trains, booking 1-2 weeks ahead is recommended, especially during holidays. You can pick up tickets at the station by showing your passport and booking confirmation.
faq.a12: Yes, DiDi has an English interface option in its app. Open Settings → Language → Select English. Note that you'll need a Chinese phone number or WeChat account to register and book rides. The app works throughout major cities and is generally cheaper than taxis.
faq.q13: Can I use Google Maps in China?
faq.a13: No, Google Maps is blocked in China. Use Amap (高德地图) instead— it's excellent, has English support, and works offline. Baidu Maps is another option. Both are much better for Chinese roads and addresses than Google Maps would be anyway.

destinations.title: Destinations - China Guide
destinations.metaDesc: Explore China's most captivating cities and destinations. From ancient capitals to modern metropolises.
destinations.heading: Destinations
destinations.subtitle: Explore China's Most Captivating Cities

beijing.title: Beijing Travel Guide - China Guide
beijing.metaDesc: Complete travel guide to Beijing: Forbidden City, Great Wall, Tiananmen Square, and more. Tips on visa, transport, and booking.
beijing.name: Beijing
beijing.tagline: Capital of Empires • Heart of China
beijing.whyVisitP1: Beijing is the cultural heart of China—a city where 3,000 years of history meets a modern metropolis. As the capital, it houses the magnificent Forbidden City, the symbolic Tiananmen Square, and the awe-inspiring Great Wall. For first-time visitors to China, Beijing is often the starting point of their journey.
beijing.whyVisitP2: The city offers world-class historical sites, incredible cuisine from Peking duck to traditional royal dishes, and a transportation system that's foreigner-friendly. English signage is common in major tourist areas, making navigation easier than many other Chinese cities.
beijing.gettingAroundP: Beijing's metro system is extensive, clean, and affordable—buy a metro card at any station. Taxis are cheap (¥10-30 for most trips) and Didi works well with English interface. For the Great Wall, take a guided tour or the train to Badaling.
beijing.didiTip: Didi: Use English interface, supports international phones
beijing.bookTripP: Find the best deals on hotels, flights, and train tickets through our trusted partner Trip.com.
beijing.forbiddenCityDesc: The imperial palace complex that served as the home of Chinese emperors for nearly 500 years. With 980 buildings and 8,000+ rooms, plan at least 3-4 hours to explore this UNESCO World Heritage site.
beijing.greatWallDesc: The most accessible section of the iconic Great Wall, about 80km from central Beijing. Take a cable car for easier access. Visit early morning to avoid crowds.
beijing.templeDesc: A beautiful complex where emperors once prayed for good harvests. The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests is one of Beijing's most photographed buildings.
beijing.summerDesc: The former royal garden featuring Kunming Lake and Longevity Hill. A peaceful retreat from the busy city with beautiful boat rides across the lake.
beijing.day1Content: Tiananmen Square → Forbidden City → Jingshan Park (sunset views)
beijing.day2Content: Great Wall (Mutianyu or Badaling) → Olympic Park (Bird's Nest exterior)
beijing.day3Content: Temple of Heaven → Summer Palace → Wangfujing Street (shopping & food)

shanghai.title: Shanghai Travel Guide - China Guide
shanghai.metaDesc: Your complete Shanghai travel guide. The Bund, Yu Garden, Pudong skyline, and more.
shanghai.name: Shanghai
shanghai.tagline: City of the Future • Modern Metropolis
shanghai.whyVisitP1: Shanghai is China's most international city—a dazzling metropolis where Art Deco towers stand alongside ancient temples, and the futuristic Pudong skyline rivals anywhere in the world. It's the perfect introduction to modern China while retaining its unique colonial heritage along the Bund.
shanghai.whyVisitP2: The city is exceptionally foreigner-friendly with extensive English signage, international dining options, and world-class infrastructure. Whether you want to explore historic Yu Garden, shop on Nanjing Road, or enjoy a cocktail atop the world's highest bars, Shanghai delivers.
shanghai.theBundDesc: The iconic waterfront promenade with 52 buildings of different architectural styles. Best viewed at night when the Pudong lights illuminate.
shanghai.pearlTowerDesc: Shanghai's most recognizable landmark with observation decks offering panoramic city views. The sphere at 268m is unforgettable.
shanghai.yuGardenDesc: A classical Chinese garden in the heart of the old city, featuring pavilions, bridges, and lotus pools from the Ming Dynasty.
shanghai.nanjingRoadDesc: The world's busiest shopping street with over 600 retailers. From luxury brands to local shops, there's something for everyone.
shanghai.bookTripP: Find hotels, flights, and trains through Trip.com.

xian.title: Xi'an Travel Guide - China Guide
xian.metaDesc: Xi'an travel guide: Terracotta Army, Ancient City Wall, Muslim Quarter, and the Silk Road legacy.
xian.name: Xi'an
xian.whyVisitP1: Xi'an is one of China's ancient capitals and the starting point of the Silk Road. It's home to one of the most significant archaeological discoveries in history—the Terracotta Army—and retains much of its medieval city walls and atmosphere. The city offers a compelling journey through 3,000 years of Chinese civilization.
xian.terracottaDesc: The mausoleum of China's first emperor, with over 8,000 life-sized clay soldiers. One of the most extraordinary archaeological sites in the world.
xian.cityWallDesc: Cycle or walk along the best-preserved ancient city wall in China. The 14km circuit offers excellent views of the old and new city.
xian.muslimQuarterDesc: A vibrant food street with hundreds of vendors selling lamb burgers, roujamo, and local snacks. Come hungry.

chengdu.title: Chengdu Travel Guide - China Guide
chengdu.metaDesc: Chengdu travel guide: Giant Pandas, Sichuan cuisine, Leshan Giant Buddha, and tea culture.
chengdu.name: Chengdu
chengdu.whyVisitP1: Chengdu is the gateway to western China and one of the most livable cities in the country. Famous for its giant panda breeding centers, legendary Sichuan cuisine (the birthplace of mapo tofu and hot pot), and a laid-back culture that revolves around tea houses and mahjong.
chengdu.whyVisitP2: The city makes an excellent base for trips to the Great Wall of China (Sichuan section), Jiuzhaigou Valley, or the Tibetan plateau. More relaxed than Beijing or Shanghai, it's perfect for slowing down and enjoying the local lifestyle.
chengdu.pandaDesc: The world's largest panda breeding center with over 20 giant pandas, plus red pandas. Get here by metro Line 3 to Panda Avenue Station, then bus or taxi. Arrive before 9:00 AM to catch the feeding sessions when pandas are most active. The nursery area lets you see cubs under 1 year old. Allow 3-4 hours minimum. Spring (March-May) offers the best weather and pandas are often outdoors. Summer can be hot and pandas spend more time in air-conditioned indoor areas.
chengdu.jinliDesc: One of the oldest commercial streets in China, dating back 1,800 years to the Three Kingdoms era. Located adjacent to Wuhousi Shrine, this 550-meter pedestrian street features over 100 shops selling Sichuan crafts, teas, and specialties. Must-try snacks:三大炮 (glutinous rice balls rolled in powdered sugar), 张飞牛肉 (dried beef slices), and 四川冰粉 (grass jelly with osmanthus syrup). Evening performances feature Sichuan opera face-changing acts—arrive at the main square by 7:30 PM for good seats. The street connects seamlessly to Wuhousi Shrine, making it easy to combine both attractions.
chengdu.peoplesParkDesc: The iconic birthplace of Chengdu's tea culture, where locals have gathered for mahjong and tea since the 1940s. The park features two famous tea houses:Mai Yao Tea House (birthplace of the unique cover碗 tea ceremony) and Heming Tea House (known for traditional cupping method with long-spout copper kettle). Rent a bamboo chair for ¥15-30, order a pot of jasmine tea (¥10-20), and watch the century-old tradition of Chinese tea preparation. On weekends, look for the matchmaking corner where parents post resumes seeking spouses for their children. The park also has a beautiful lotus pond, ancient banyan trees, and a small amusement park section.
chengdu.kuanzhaiDesc: Chengdu's most Instagram-worthy ancient street district consists of two parallel alleys—Kuan Alley (Wide Alley) and Zhai Alley (Narrow Alley)—dating back to the Qing Dynasty. Kuan Alley preserves traditional courtyard homes now converted to upscale tea houses, boutique shops, and Sichuan opera stages. Zhai Alley is livelier with street food stalls selling三大炮 (three throws),糖油果子, and辣椒油. The best time to visit is 5:00-7:00 PM when the lanterns light up. Look for the giant teapot sculpture at the entrance as a photo spot. Most shops open 10:00 AM onward.
chengdu.wuhousiDesc: Chengdu's most culturally significant site, built in 223 AD to honor Liu Bei, leader of the Shu Kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period. The complex spans 37 acres with over 100 buildings including the main hall featuring a 5-meter statue of Liu Bei, and separate halls for Zhuge Liang (the famous strategist) and Guan Yu. The gardens are considered among the finest in Sichuan with ancient mulberry trees, lotus ponds, and corridors connecting the halls. Look for the famous Crossroads of Heroes inscription—legend says if you can find your zodiac animal among the 14 bronze sheep, you'll have good fortune. A unique feature is the red walls and golden roof tiles perfect for photography.
chengdu.dujiangyanDesc: A UNESCO World Heritage site and engineering marvel that has controlled the Min River and protected Chengdu from floods for 2,200 years. Built by Li Bing in 256 BC, it's still functioning today as the main water source for 530,000 acres of farmland. Key features: the Fish Mouth levee (yue嘴分水鱼嘴) that divides the river, the Flying Dragon Weir (飞沙堰) that controls flooding, and Baopingkou (宝瓶口) that regulates water flow. Take the scenic route following the water flow: start at Minjiang Bridge, cross to the Fish Mouth, walk along the outer riverbank to Flying Dragon Weir, then through the shaded Valley of Pinqu (平泉峡谷) to Baopingkou. For the best views, climb to the Erwang Temple viewpoint. Combine with Mount Qingcheng for a perfect day trip.
chengdu.foodIntro: Chengdu is the birthplace of Sichuan cuisine, famous for its signature málà (numbing-spicy) flavor. Here are must-try dishes and where to find them:
chengdu.hotpotDesc: Chengdu invented what the world calls hot pot—but local versions are different from what you find abroad. The signature is 麻辣 (málà): spicy red broth combined with Sichuan peppercorns creating that unique tingly-numbing sensation. Unlike the beef-heavy versions overseas, Chengdu hot pot features thinly-sliced lamb, fresh river fish, lotus root, enoki mushrooms, and most importantly—fresh tofu skin (豆皮). Recommended restaurants:海底捞 (Haidilao, with English menu and excellent service),老码头 (Old Dock, historic 1940s establishment),蜀大侠 (Shu Daxia, affordable and authentic). Expect 2-3 hours for a proper hot pot meal. Pro tip: Dip the beef in egg yolk before cooking for extra tender meat.
chengdu.chuanchuanDesc: Sichuan's answer to hot pot on sticks! This affordable, casual street food lets you pick from hundreds of skewers displayed in refrigerated cases—everything from fresh vegetables to tripe, tofu skin, lotus root, quail eggs, and small meat portions. The cook prepares your selections in seconds in a bubbling麻辣 broth. You pay by the number of sticks: typically ¥1-3 per stick. The best spots are along Jinli Street and behind People's Park. Try:小郡肝 (chicken knee cartilage, the most popular),嫩牛肉 (tender beef slices), and藕片 (lotus root, best when slightly spicy). Come hungry—it's hard to stop at just a few rounds!
chengdu.mapoDesc: Born in Chengdu in 1862 by a Qing Dynasty restaurant owner whose face was marked with smallpox scars (mapo means pockmarked grandmother), this dish features silken tofu in a spicy red sauce with minced pork and Sichuan peppercorns. The magic is in the sauce—a complex combination of doubanjiang (fermented chili bean paste), Sichuan peppercorn oil, and starch thickening that creates the signature numbing-spicy sensation. Best found at:陈麻婆豆腐 (Chen Mapo Tofu, the original restaurant, operating since 1862),夫妻肺片 (actually beef offal in spicy sauce, not the misnamed husband and wife lung slices), or any local restaurant. Eaten with steamed rice—the rich sauce coats each grain perfectly.
chengdu.dandanDesc: Named after the pole carriers (担担) who sold this street food throughout Chengdu's alleys, dan dan noodles consist of thin wheat noodles in a complex sesame-peanut broth, topped with spicy chili oil, minced pork, and pickled vegetables. The balance of richness from sesame paste, heat from chili, and acidity from pickles creates layers of flavor in every bite. Near People's Park, follow the crowd to:担担面 (Dan Dan House, the most famous, operating since 1936), just look for the small shop with red lanterns. Breakfast is the best time—locals consider it the proper way to start the day.
chengdu.tangyuanDesc: Though available year-round, tangyuan is especially traditional during the Lantern Festival (lunar 15th day of new year). These small glutinous rice balls filled with black sesame paste, red bean, or meat stuffing are served in sweet osmanthus soup. In Chengdu, they often come with añato seeds giving them a golden color. The smooth, chewy texture paired with the subtle sweetness of the soup represents the rounded completeness of family reunions in Chinese culture. Find them at:Luocheng Old Snacks (老成都小吃) near Jinli Street, or any dim sum restaurant.
chengdu.tipsDesc: Chengdu restaurants typically don't require reservations except for famous hot pot chains (Haidilao accepts bookings via app). English menus are rare outside tourist areas—use Google Translate's camera feature to scan Chinese menus. Most restaurants open at 10-11 AM for lunch and don't serve dinner until 5 PM. Sichuan peppercorns cause a tingly numbness that can last 20-30 minutes—this is normal! Pace yourself: start with mild dishes and build up to the real麻辣 experience. Tipping is not expected. Look for establishments with local customers—that's how you know it's good.
chengdu.day1Content: Morning: Giant Panda Base → Afternoon: Kuanzhai Alley → Evening: Jinli Street + Hot Pot dinner
chengdu.day2Content: Morning: People's Park tea experience → Wuhousi Shrine → Afternoon: Shopping at Chunxi Road → Evening: Show at Jinli Street
chengdu.day3Content: Day trip to Dujiangyan + Mount Qingcheng OR Giant Panda Base (repeat morning session)

zhangjiajie.title: Zhangjiajie Travel Guide - China Guide
zhangjiajie.metaDesc: Zhangjiajie travel guide: Avatar Mountains, glass bridges, Zhangjiajie National Forest Park.
zhangjiajie.name: Zhangjiajie
zhangjiajie.whyVisitP1: Zhangjiajie is home to one of China's most spectacular natural landscapes—the towering sandstone pillars of Wulingyuan that inspired the floating mountains in Avatar. This UNESCO World Heritage site offers some of the most dramatic scenery in China, with mist-shrouded peaks, deep valleys, and the world's tallest glass bridge.
zhangjiajie.whyVisitP2: Located in remote Hunan Province, Zhangjiajie requires more effort to reach but rewards visitors with unforgettable hiking, photography, and natural beauty. Best visited with a local guide due to limited English signage.
zhangjiajie.wulingyuanDesc: The main park featuring thousands of sandstone pillars, deep ravines, and pristine forests. Takes 2-3 days to fully explore.
zhangjiajie.glassBridgeDesc: The world's longest and highest glass-bottomed bridge, suspended 300 meters above a canyon. Not for the faint-hearted!

chongqing.title: Chongqing Travel Guide - China Guide
chongqing.metaDesc: Chongqing travel guide: Mountain city, hot pot capital, Three Gorges, mysterious Dali.
chongqing.name: Chongqing
chongqing.whyVisitP1: Chongqing is one of China's most unique cities—a mountain metropolis built on dramatic terrain where the Yangtze and Jialing Rivers meet. Known as the "Hot Pot Capital," it's a food lover's paradise with an intense, spicy cuisine that defines the region. The city is also the gateway to Three Gorges and a booming modern urban center with futuristic architecture.
chongqing.whyVisitP2: Unlike the flat terrain of most Chinese cities, Chongqing is built on hills with stunning night views, ancient streets, and natural hot springs. The locals are known for their resilience and hospitality, making visitors feel welcome in this vibrant metropolis.
chongqing.hongyaDongDesc: A stunning riverside complex built on cliffsides with traditional stilted houses. Best viewed at night when illuminated. The largest tourist attraction in China by visitor count.
chongqing.hotPotDesc: Chongqing invented the numbing and spicy hot pot. Try Erlang Hot Pot Street or any local restaurant for an authentic experience with málà (numbing-spicy) broth.
chongqing.threeGorgesDesc: Learn about the Three Gorges dam project and the region's history, culture, and natural landscapes through excellent exhibits and artifacts.
chongqing.cableCarDesc: Cross the Yangtze River by cable car—a unique experience offering panoramic views of the mountain city skyline and the river below.
chongqing.day1Content: Hongya Dong → Yangtze River Cable Car → Jiefangbei CBD → Hot pot dinner
chongqing.day2Content: Three Gorges Museum → Ciqikou Ancient Street → Eling Park (sunset views)
chongqing.gettingAroundP: Chongqing's terrain is unique—many streets are interconnected through overpasses and underground pathways. The metro system is extensive and can feel like a roller coaster with underground stations and elevated lines. Taxis and Didi are cheap and convenient.
chongqing.cableCarTip: Cable Car: ¥20 one-way, scenic river crossing
chongqing.bookTripP: Find the best deals on hotels, flights, and Yangtze River cruises through our trusted partner Trip.com.

visa.title: China Visa Guide for Americans - China Guide
visa.metaDesc: Complete visa guide for Americans traveling to China. Visa requirements, application process, 144-hour transit visa, and more.
visa.heading: Visa Guide
visa.subtitle: Everything you need to know about entering China
visa.needVisaP: Yes, most American citizens need a visa to enter China. However, there are some exceptions and convenient options available.
visa.touristVisaP: For most tourists, you'll need to apply for a Tourist Visa (L Visa). Here's what you need:
visa.transitP: If you're transiting through specific cities, you may qualify for a 144-hour visa-free transit:
visa.transitItem1: Available in: Shanghai, Beijing, Guangzhou, Chengdu, Chongqing, and more
visa.transitItem2: Must enter and exit within 144 hours (6 days)
visa.transitItem3: Must have a ticket to a third country
visa.transitItem4: Only available for specific nationalities including US citizens
visa.transitNote: Note: This is NOT available for Hong Kong entry—only mainland China.
visa.multiP: US citizens may qualify for a 10-year multiple entry visa which allows stays of up to 60-120 days per visit. Requirements are the same as tourist visa but with longer validity.
visa.whereP: Apply at your nearest Chinese Embassy or Consulate. In the US:
visa.tip1: Apply at least 6-8 weeks before your trip
visa.tip2: Visa validity starts from issue date, not entry date
visa.tip3: Keep your visa in your passport— Photocopy everything!
visa.tip4: Overstaying can result in fines and future visa denials
visa.tip5: Visa requirements can change— always check current regulations
visa.relatedP: See our FAQs for common questions about China visas.

payment.title: China Payment Guide - Alipay & WeChat Pay - China Guide
payment.metaDesc: How to use Alipay and WeChat Pay as a foreigner in China. Set up international cards and navigate cashless China.
payment.heading: Payment Guide
payment.subtitle: How to pay like a local in China
payment.cashlessP1: China is essentially a cashless society. Mobile payment through Alipay and WeChat Pay dominates—not just at restaurants and shops, but also metro stations, street vendors, and hotels. As a foreigner, you'll need to set up at least one of these apps before arriving.
payment.cashlessP2: The good news: Both apps now support international credit cards, making it easier than ever for foreign tourists.
payment.alipayIntro: Alipay is the most widely accepted mobile payment app in China.
payment.alipaySetup1: Download Alipay app (App Store / Google Play)
payment.alipaySetup4: Scan your passport and complete verification
payment.alipaySetup5: Link your international Visa, Mastercard, or Amex card
payment.alipaySetup6: Add funds to your digital wallet (required for some merchants)
payment.alipayTip1: Look for the Alipay QR code at checkout
payment.alipayTip2: Open the app, tap "Scan" and scan the merchant's code
payment.alipayTip3: Confirm the amount and enter your payment password
payment.alipayTip4: Some merchants may require you to load money to the app balance
payment.wechatIntro: WeChat Pay is integrated with WeChat messenger, used by over a billion Chinese users.
payment.wechatSetup3: Go to Settings → Payment → Add Card
payment.wechatCompareTitle: When to Use WeChat vs Alipay
payment.wechatCompare1: Alipay: Better for tourists, wider acceptance, more English-friendly
payment.wechatCompare2: WeChat Pay: Useful for WeChat-based services, some exclusive discounts
payment.cashIntro: While cashless is preferred, you should still bring some cash:
payment.cash2: Exchange at airports, hotels, or banks
payment.cash3: ATMs accepting international cards are common in cities
payment.cash4: Rural areas may still require some cash
payment.cash5: Small tips are not customary (but appreciated)
payment.creditIntro: International credit cards are NOT widely accepted in China. Many smaller merchants, restaurants, and shops only take Alipay or WeChat Pay. International cards mainly work at:
payment.creditConclusion: Bottom line: Set up Alipay before arrival—it's essential.

network.title: China Internet Guide - VPN & eSIM - China Guide
network.metaDesc: Internet access in China for travelers. VPN recommendations, eSIM options, and Great Firewall guide.
network.heading: Internet & VPN Guide
network.subtitle: Stay connected in China
network.firewallP1: China operates one of the most extensive internet filtering systems in the world, commonly known as the "Great Firewall." Many Western websites and services are blocked, including:
network.firewallConclusion: You will not be able to access these services without a VPN.
network.vpnIntro: A reliable VPN is essential for most travelers. We recommend:
network.vpnWarning: Important: Download and set up your VPN before arriving in China. Once you're in China, it may be difficult to access VPN websites to download the apps.
network.roamingP: Check with your home carrier about international roaming plans. US carriers often charge $10-15/day for data in China, which can add up quickly.
network.esimIntro: For a more convenient option, consider an eSIM data plan:
network.esim1: Airalo: eSIM for 190+ countries, China data from $5
network.esim3: Hong Kong eSIM: Often works in mainland China
network.physicalP: You can buy a Chinese SIM card at airports (China Mobile, China Unicom, Telecom). You'll need your passport. Data plans are affordable (¥30-100 for several GB).
network.worksIntro: Many services function normally in China:
network.tip1: Set up before arrival: Install your VPN and apps in your home country
network.tip2: Download offline maps: Use Amap or Maps.me for offline navigation
network.tip3: Keep multiple options: Have both your VPN and a local data solution
network.tip4: Save important info offline: Hotel addresses, train tickets, etc.

apps.title: Essential China Travel Apps - China Guide
apps.metaDesc: Must-download apps for traveling in China. Navigation, translation, food delivery, and transport.
apps.heading: Essential Apps
apps.subtitle: Must-download apps for traveling in China
apps.downloadP: All these apps are available on iOS App Store and Google Play. Download them before arriving in China since Google Play is not accessible from within China.
apps.alipayDesc: The most essential app for China. Use it for everything from street food to metro rides. Supports international credit cards.
apps.wechatDesc: More than just messaging—WeChat Pay, mini-programs, and integrated services. Essential for communicating with locals.
apps.didiDesc: China's dominant ride-hailing app, similar to Uber. English interface available. Must have a Chinese phone number or WeChat for verification.
apps.amapDesc: The best navigation app for China, with English support. Use for driving, walking, or public transit directions. Works offline.
apps.metroDesc: Shows metro routes in major Chinese cities with English. Essential for navigating subway systems in Beijing, Shanghai, etc.
apps.appleDesc: Built into iOS, works offline for Chinese-English translation. Simple and effective.
apps.baiduDesc: Excellent translation app with photo translation (point camera at Chinese text), conversation mode, and voice translation.
apps.tripDesc: All-in-one booking app for flights, hotels, trains, and cars. English interface, accepts international cards.
apps.railDesc: Official app for booking China Railway high-speed train tickets. English version available.
apps.commsP: The "app for everything" in China. Messaging, voice calls, video calls, payments, mini-programs, and more. Download before arrival.
apps.vpnDesc: Don't forget your VPN! We recommend ExpressVPN, NordVPN, or similar for accessing blocked services.

firstTime.title: First Time in China - 7 Day Itinerary - China Guide
firstTime.metaDesc: The ultimate first-time China itinerary. 7 days covering Beijing and Xi'an with the Great Wall, Forbidden City, and Terracotta Army.
firstTime.heading: First Time in China
firstTime.subtitle: 7 Days • 2 Cities • The Ultimate Introduction
firstTime.routeP2: This classic route is perfect for first-time visitors who want to experience China's most iconic attractions. You'll walk where emperors once walked, stand on the Great Wall, and come face-to-face with the Terracotta Army.
firstTime.day1p: Settle into your hotel and recover from jet lag. Take a evening stroll around your neighborhood and sample your first Chinese meal. If you're feeling energetic, head to the CBD for a view of the modern skyline.
firstTime.day2p: Start early at Tiananmen Square (free entry), then enter the Forbidden City (¥60). Plan 3-4 hours here—it's enormous. Climb Jingshan Park for sunset views over the palace rooftops.
firstTime.day3p: Take a day trip to Mutianyu or Badaling section of the Great Wall (¥65). Cable car available at Mutianyu. Return to Beijing in the evening and rest—tomorrow is another big day.
firstTime.day4h: Day 4: Temple of Heaven & Summer Palace
firstTime.day4p: Visit the Temple of Heaven (¥34) in the morning to see locals doing tai chi. In the afternoon, explore the Summer Palace (¥30)—take a boat ride on Kunming Lake.
firstTime.day5p: Visit Olympic Park to see the Bird's Nest and Water Cube from outside (free). Afternoon for shopping at Wangfujing or Silk Street. Evening: try authentic Peking duck.
firstTime.day6h: Day 6: Beijing → Xi'an by High-Speed Rail
firstTime.day6p: Take the morning high-speed train to Xi'an (4.5 hours, ¥515 second class). Afternoon explore Xi'an City Wall (¥54)—rent a bike to cycle the 14km circuit. Evening: explore the vibrant Muslim Quarter food street.
firstTime.day7p: Morning visit to the Terracotta Army (¥120), one of the greatest archaeological discoveries. Afternoon return to Xi'an airport or train station for departure.
firstTime.bookP: Find the best deals on hotels, flights, and train tickets.

china7days.title: China in 7 Days - 3 Cities Itinerary - China Guide
china7days.metaDesc: 7-day China itinerary covering 3 cities. Fast-paced highlights for time-conscious travelers.
china7days.heading: China in 7 Days
china7days.subtitle: 7 Days • 3 Cities • Fast-Paced Highlights
china7days.routeP1: Beijing (2 days) → Flight (2 hours) → Shanghai (2 days) → Flight (2 hours) → Xi'an (3 days)
china7days.routeP2: This itinerary maximizes your time in China by flying between cities. Best suited for travelers with limited time who want to see the country's most iconic landmarks.

rail.title: China High-Speed Rail Journey - 14 Days - China Guide
rail.metaDesc: 14-day China itinerary by high-speed rail. Shanghai, Nanjing, Suzhou, Hangzhou, and beyond.
rail.heading: High-Speed Rail Journey
rail.subtitle: 14 Days • 5 Cities • Explore China by Train
rail.routeP1: Shanghai (3 days) → Train (25 min) → Suzhou (2 days) → Train (1 hour) → Nanjing (2 days) → Train (3 hours) → Hangzhou (2 days) → Train (1 hour) → Wuyuan (2 days)
rail.routeP2: Experience China's well-connected high-speed rail network on this scenic journey through the Yangtze Delta. Each city offers unique character—from Shanghai's modernity to Wuyuan's ancient villages.
